content:
# Genesis Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2v–3r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_003_page_001.jpg, GEN_003_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**3:1** Now the serpent was more cunning than any beast of the field that the LORD God had made. And it said to the woman, "Is it indeed so that God has said, 'You shall not eat from any tree of the garden'?"

**3:2** And the woman said to the serpent, "From the fruit of the trees of the garden we may eat —

**3:3** but from the fruit of the tree that is in the midst of the garden, God has said, 'You shall not eat from it, and you shall not touch it, lest you die.'"

**3:4** And the serpent said to the woman, "You shall not surely die.

**3:5** For God knows that in the day you eat from it, your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil."

**3:6** And the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable for gaining wisdom. She took from its fruit and ate, and she gave also to her husband, who was with her, and he ate.

**3:7** Then the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked. And they sewed fig leaves together and made loin-coverings for themselves.

**3:8** And they heard the sound of the LORD God walking about in the garden in the cool of the day, and the man and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God among the trees of the garden.

**3:9** And the LORD God called to the man, and said to him, "Where are you?"

**3:10** And he said, "I heard your sound in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked — and I hid myself."

**3:11** And He said, "Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree from which I commanded you not to eat?"

**3:12** And the man said, "The woman whom You gave to be with me — she gave me from the tree, and I ate."

**3:13** And the LORD God said to the woman, "What is this you have done?" And the woman said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."

**3:14** And the LORD God said to the serpent:

> "Because you have done this,
> cursed are you above all the livestock
> and above every beast of the field.
> On your belly you shall go,
> and dust you shall eat
> all the days of your life.

**3:15** And I will put enmity between you and the woman,
> and between your seed and her seed.
> He shall bruise your head,
> and you shall bruise his heel."

**3:16** To the woman He said:

> "I will greatly multiply your pain and your conception.
> In pain you shall bring forth children.
> And your desire shall be for your husband,
> and he shall rule over you."

**3:17** And to the man He said:

> "Because you listened to the voice of your wife
> and ate from the tree about which I commanded you,
> saying, 'You shall not eat from it' —
> cursed is the ground on your account.
> In painful toil you shall eat from it
> all the days of your life.

**3:18** Thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you,
> and you shall eat the plant of the field.

**3:19** By the sweat of your face
> you shall eat bread,
> until you return to the ground —
> for from it you were taken.
> For you are dust,
> and to dust you shall return."

**3:20** And the man called his wife's name Eve, for she was the mother of all living.

**3:21** And the LORD God made garments of skin for the man and for his wife, and He clothed them.

**3:22** And the LORD God said, "Behold, the man has become like one of Us, to know good and evil. And now, lest he stretch out his hand and take also from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever —"

**3:23** So the LORD God sent him out from the garden of Eden, to work the ground from which he was taken.

**3:24** He drove out the man, and He stationed at the east of the garden of Eden the cherubim and the flame of the revolving sword, to guard the way to the tree of life.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### On the Manuscript

Both folios of the Leningrad Codex (B19A) were examined directly in the manuscript images. Folio 2v (page 1) presents the three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks that characterize the manuscript throughout. The text runs in square Masoretic script with Masorah Parva visible in the column margins. Genesis 3 begins on folio 2v following Gen 2:25, with the famous *'arum*/*'arummim* wordplay bridging the two chapters — visible in the manuscript as the close proximity of the *'arummim* ending of chapter 2 and the *'arum* opening of chapter 3. Folio 3r (page 2) carries the chapter through the divine curses, the expulsion, and the closing image of the cherubim and revolving sword. The chapter boundary is a later canonical division; the Masoretic paragraph structure (setumah/petuchah markers, evidenced by spacing in the columns) divides the text into units corresponding roughly to: the temptation (3:1–7), the confrontation (3:8–13), the curses (3:14–19), and the aftermath (3:20–24). The handwriting, vocalization, and cantillation are consistent and unambiguous throughout. No manuscript anomalies affecting the translation were observed.

---

### Note 1 — 3:1: *'Arum* — "Cunning" and the Bridge from Genesis 2:25

All three translators — EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — independently identified the *'arum*/*'arummim* wordplay as the most important literary feature bridging chapters 2 and 3. They diverged only in their English rendering: EMET preserved *'arum* with a bracketed gloss "[cunning/shrewd]"; LOGOS chose "crafty"; PNEUMA chose "cunning" (in the superlative, "most cunning").

SOPHIA adopts "cunning" (comparative: "more cunning than any beast") for the following reasons: (a) "cunning" carries the dual register of intelligence and predatory intent that both the Hebrew *'arum* and the LXX's *phronimōtatos* require; (b) unlike "crafty," which in modern English has an almost exclusively negative moral valence, "cunning" preserves the positive-register ambiguity of *'arum* — the same word used positively of the wise in Proverbs; (c) "more cunning than any beast" follows the comparative construction of the MT (*'arum mikkol*) rather than the LXX's superlative, which over-intensifies.

The wordplay with *'arummim* ("naked," 2:25) is untranslatable in English. It is noted here and in the Disagreement Log for scholarly attention. The Leningrad Codex manuscript confirms the two distinct vocalizations: *'arummim* (with *hateph-patah*) in 2:25 and *'arum* (with *qamets* under the *'ayin*, *shureq* after the *resh*) in 3:1.

### Note 2 — 3:1: *'Af ki* — "Is It Indeed So That"

All three translators agreed on the interrogative, subtly incredulous force of *'af ki*. LOGOS's formal rendering "Is it indeed so that God has said" is adopted here, as it preserves: (a) the genuine interrogative structure, (b) the edge of irony/surprise, and (c) the sense that the serpent is not outright denying but subtly misquoting — converting God's one exception into a total prohibition, to draw the woman into elaborating the command. PNEUMA's "Did God really say" is also excellent and idiomatic, but slightly flattens the formal structure of *'af ki*. EMET's "Indeed, [is it true] that" is too parenthetically cumbersome. LOGOS's rendering is adopted.

### Note 3 — 3:3: The Woman's Addition — "You Shall Not Touch It"

All three translators rendered this faithfully as the woman's addition to God's command (which in 2:17 said nothing about touching). No translator softened or explained it away in the translation text itself. SOPHIA preserves it exactly as stated. The addition — whether a "fence around the Torah" (rabbinic reading) or an anxious over-precision — is the text's own narrative detail. The reader must encounter it without editorial padding.

### Note 4 — 3:4–5: The Serpent's Emphatic Negation

EMET rendered the emphatic *lo' mot temutun* most literally: "Not dying you-shall-die." LOGOS offered "You shall not surely die" (retaining "surely" as marker of the doubled construction). PNEUMA chose the flat "You will not die," arguing for dramatic bluntness. SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's "You shall not surely die," which: (a) preserves the emphatic construction via "surely," echoing the doubled infinitive absolute; (b) maintains the formal register consistent with the divine speech that follows; and (c) keeps grammatical parallelism with the rendering of God's warning in 2:17 ("you shall surely die"). The serpent's speech directly inverts God's grammar — the translation honors this structural confrontation.

### Note 5 — 3:5: "Like God" vs. "Like Gods"

This is one of two theologically significant divergence points in the chapter. EMET and LOGOS both render *ke-Elohim* as "like God" (singular), following the consistent use of *Elohim* as singular referent throughout the narrative. PNEUMA also renders "like God," noting the ambiguity. All three agree: SOPHIA adopts "like God."

The LXX Vaticanus reads *hōs theoi* ("like gods," plural). This is noted as a live alternative supported by: (a) the morphologically plural *Elohim*; (b) the divine plural in 3:22 ("like one of Us"); (c) the possible divine-council register. However, the MT's contextual usage and the consistent narrative pattern of this section support the singular. "Like God" is adopted.

### Note 6 — 3:6: "Desirable for Gaining Wisdom"

EMET rendered *nechmad ha-'ets le-haskil* as "desirable the-tree to-make-[one]-wise." LOGOS rendered "desirable to make one wise." PNEUMA rendered "desirable for gaining wisdom." SOPHIA adopts PNEUMA's rendering: "desirable for gaining wisdom" — the most natural English formulation of the purposive infinitive (*le-haskil*, Hiphil of *sakal*) that preserves the intellectual aspiration dimension. The threefold structure of the woman's evaluation (physical / aesthetic / intellectual) is preserved in the translation: "good for food... a delight to the eyes... desirable for gaining wisdom."

EMET's note on *'immah* ("with her") is of high importance: the man was present throughout the encounter. SOPHIA preserves "who was with her" (following PNEUMA's word order and LOGOS's phrasing) rather than EMET's "with-her-man," to maintain idiomatic English while keeping the moral weight of the man's silent presence.

### Note 7 — 3:7: "Loin-Coverings" vs. "Coverings" vs. "Coverings"

EMET rendered *chagorot* as "loin-coverings [*chagorot*]." LOGOS rendered "coverings." PNEUMA rendered "coverings." SOPHIA adopts "loin-coverings," following EMET's more precise rendering: the word *chagorot* specifically denotes a girded covering for the lower body (from *chagar*, "to gird"), and the specificity is narratively significant — it points to the precise location of the shame and sets up the contrast with YHWH God's full garments of skin in 3:21. LOGOS and PNEUMA's "coverings" is accurate but loses the anatomical precision.

### Note 8 — 3:8: *Le-ruach ha-yom* — "In the Cool of the Day"

This is a significant divergence point. EMET rendered "in-[the]-breeze of-the-day" (most literal). LOGOS rendered "in the wind of the day" (formally literal). PNEUMA rendered "in the cool of the day" (most evocative, follows KJV tradition). The LXX Vaticanus reads *to deilinon* ("in the afternoon/evening"), a temporal rather than atmospheric rendering.

SOPHIA adopts "in the cool of the day" for the following reasons: (a) *ruach ha-yom* in context describes the late-afternoon wind that cools the Levantine garden — "cool of the day" renders the experiential reality of this atmospheric moment; (b) PNEUMA's case that "cool of the day" is the most evocative and musically appropriate English phrase is well made; (c) "the wind of the day" (LOGOS) is accurate but cold; "the breeze of the day" (EMET) is accurate but slightly technical. The phrase "cool of the day" has its own literary dignity in English that matches the dignity of the Hebrew narrative moment.

EMET's note that *mithallekh* (Hithpael, "walking about") attributes the motion to God is correct and all translators agree. PNEUMA's "walking about" (adopted here) captures the Hithpael's iterative-reflexive force — the unhurried, habitual presence — better than LOGOS's bare "walking."

### Note 9 — 3:9: *'Ayyekkah* — "Where Are You?"

All three translators agreed. The single Hebrew word is rendered in three English words by necessity. All agreed the question is not geographic but relational — a summons, an invitation to self-disclosure. SOPHIA adopts the simple "Where are you?" shared by all three.

### Note 10 — 3:10: "I Hid Myself"

EMET: "and-I-hid-myself." LOGOS: "and I hid myself." PNEUMA: "so I hid." SOPHIA adopts LOGOS/EMET's "and I hid myself" — the reflexive force of the Hebrew *wa'ekkhave'* is preserved. PNEUMA's "so I hid" loses the reflexive.

### Note 11 — 3:12–13: The Deflections

All three translators preserved the two-stage deflection structure (man blames woman-and-God; woman blames serpent) without softening. The critical phrase "whom You gave to be with me" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "whom You-gave with-me" (EMET) is rendered here as "whom You gave to be with me" — LOGOS's formulation is adopted as the most idiomatic English that preserves the implicit accusation directed toward God. The accusatory grammar must not be softened: it is one of the most psychologically precise moments in the Hebrew narrative.

### Note 12 — 3:14–19: The Curses as Poetry

PNEUMA set the divine pronouncements in verse form. LOGOS rendered them as elevated prose. EMET rendered them as close-to-literal prose. SOPHIA adopts PNEUMA's structural decision to present 3:14–19 as poetry, for two reasons: (a) the Masoretic cantillation marks on these verses in the Leningrad Codex (visible in the manuscript images on folio 3r) follow a more measured, elevated pattern consistent with elevated speech — PNEUMA and EMET both noted this; (b) the parallelism, rhythm, and repetition of the curse formulas function as poetry in any literary sense. The Leningrad Codex's spacing and the LXX's treatment both confirm the elevated register of this passage. The translation reflects this by indented verse format for 3:14–19.

### Note 13 — 3:14: Structural Asymmetry of the Curses

EMET's note on the crucial asymmetry is preserved: the serpent is directly cursed (*'arur 'attah*, "cursed are you"); the ground is later cursed (*'arurah ha-'adamah*, 3:17); but the woman and the man are not directly cursed. They receive descriptions of painful consequence, not the curse-word *'arur* applied to them. SOPHIA's translation preserves this exactly by reserving "cursed" for 3:14 (serpent) and 3:17 (ground) only.

### Note 14 — 3:15: *Shuph* — "Bruise" and the Protoevangelium

This is the most theologically contested verse in the chapter and the most significant divergence among the three translators.

EMET: "he will-bruise you [the]-head / and-you will-bruise him [the]-heel" — both instances rendered "bruise," preserving Hebrew symmetry.

LOGOS: "He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel" — both "bruise," preserving symmetry.

PNEUMA: "He will crush your head, and you will strike his heel" — *asymmetric* rendering: "crush" for the first instance, "strike" for the second, to reflect the different lethality of head-strike vs. heel-strike.

SOPHIA adopts LOGOS and EMET's symmetric rendering: "He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel." Reasons: (a) the Hebrew uses the same verb (*shuph*) for both actions — the asymmetry of outcome (head vs. heel) does sufficient theological work without introducing an asymmetry of vocabulary that the Hebrew does not support; (b) the Vulgate's *conteret*/*insidiaberis* asymmetry, and its Marian *ipsa* variant, are well-documented distortions of the MT; SOPHIA will not replicate them; (c) "bruise" is the most defensible English rendering of the rare *shuph* — neither as weak as "watch over" (LXX *tereō*) nor as strong as "crush," which is unsupported by the root's attested range. The LXX's *tērēsei* ("shall watch for") is noted as an ancient alternative but not followed.

The masculine pronoun *hu'* ("he") is preserved throughout. The Vaticanus LXX confirms the masculine — against the Vetus Latina *ipsa* ("she") that generated the Marian reading. SOPHIA follows the MT and Vaticanus.

### Note 15 — 3:16: *'Itsabon* and *Teshukah*

**Pain:** EMET rendered *'itsabon* as "pain," noting its application equally to the woman (3:16) and the man (3:17). LOGOS rendered "pain" and "painful toil" respectively. PNEUMA rendered "labor... severe" (paraphrastic at the opening). SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS's rendering "pain" — preserving the shared root between the woman's and the man's judgments. The formal symmetry is a deliberate textual feature.

**Conception:** EMET rendered *ve-heronekh* as "and-your-conception." LOGOS rendered "in your childbearing" (collapsing conception and birth). PNEUMA omitted it as a separate element. SOPHIA follows EMET in rendering "your pain and your conception" — the MT's two separate nouns (*'itsavon* and *herayon*) name two distinct dimensions of the woman's new condition: the pain itself and the process of conception/pregnancy. Collapsing them loses a textual distinction preserved in the Leningrad Codex.

**Desire:** All three translators rendered *teshukah* as "desire." The LXX's *apostrophē* ("turning") is noted as an ancient alternative capturing the directional quality, but "desire" follows the MT and is adopted by all three translators.

**"Shall rule":** All three agreed. Adopted.

### Note 16 — 3:17: *Ba-'avurekha* — "On Your Account"

All three translators agreed that the ground is cursed "on your account" (LOGOS/PNEUMA: "because of you"). SOPHIA adopts "on your account" as slightly more literal and theologically precise — it locates the causation in Adam's action without making the phrasing merely causal.

### Note 17 — 3:19: "By the Sweat of Your Face"

EMET rendered *be-ze'at appekha* as "By-[the]-sweat of your-nostrils [face]," noting that *'af* literally means "nostril" but idiomatically means "face." LOGOS rendered "By the sweat of your face." PNEUMA rendered "By the sweat of your brow." SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's "By the sweat of your face" — it is the literal rendering of the idiomatic Hebrew (*'appekha* as "face"), it has the dignity of the passage, and it is the rendering confirmed by the LXX (*tou prosōpou sou*, "of your face"). PNEUMA's "brow" is a further step toward modern idiom but interprets rather than translates. EMET's "nostrils" is accurate but clinically precise in a way that works in a gloss but not in a flowing verse.

### Note 18 — 3:20: Chavvah/Eve

All three translators agreed on "Eve" as the English form, with EMET and PNEUMA noting the Hebrew *Chavvah* / *Havvah* and its etymology from *chai* ("living"). LOGOS noted the LXX's remarkable translation of the name as *Zōē* ("Life") rather than a transliteration. SOPHIA follows all three in using "Eve" as the established English form. The naming's placement — after the curses and before the expulsion — is preserved in the structure of the translation. PNEUMA's note on its character as "a moment of remarkable defiance and tenderness" is an apt description of the text's own quiet act.

### Note 19 — 3:22: The Broken Sentence (*Anapodoton*)

EMET identified and preserved the grammatical break: the sentence "lest he stretch out his hand... and live forever —" trails off without completing its main clause. The action of 3:23 follows immediately as if executing the unspoken conclusion. LOGOS and PNEUMA both preserved this with an em-dash. SOPHIA preserves the broken syntax with "—" at the close of 3:22. Smoothing this into a complete sentence would erase one of the passage's most dramatically significant grammatical features. All three translators agreed; SOPHIA confirms.

EMET also notes the divine plural *ke-'echad mimmenu* ("like one of Us") — confirmed by Vaticanus's *hōs heis ex hēmōn*. All three translators rendered this consistently. Adopted.

### Note 20 — 3:24: The Cherubim and the Revolving Sword

EMET rendered *lahat ha-cherev ha-mithappekhet* as "the-flame of [the]-sword the-revolving." LOGOS rendered "the flame of a sword turning itself." PNEUMA rendered "the flaming, turning sword." SOPHIA adopts "the flame of the revolving sword," which: (a) preserves *lahat* as a noun ("flame") rather than an adjective ("flaming"), following EMET and LOGOS; (b) uses "revolving" (EMET's word) which better captures the Hithpael's continuous rotational motion than "turning" (which could be a single turn); (c) is not PNEUMA's "flaming, turning" (which turns *lahat* into an adjective). The cherubim are placed "at the east of the garden of Eden" — LOGOS's rendering adopted (EMET: "from-east"; PNEUMA: "to the east"). The definite article on *ha-keruvim* ("the cherubim," as if already known to the reader) is preserved via "the cherubim."

### Note 21 — Consistency with Genesis 1–2 Terminology

The following terminological continuities with the Genesis 1–2 consensus (The SOPHIA Bible) are maintained throughout:
- "the LORD God" for YHWH Elohim — all three agreed, adopted throughout
- "man" for *ha-adam* (with article) where the man is meant as a named role; "the human" considered but "the man" maintained for narrative continuity with Genesis 2
- "made" for *'asah* (3:1, 3:21) — LOGOS confirmed; "formed" (*yatsar*) does not appear in this chapter
- "living" in 3:20 (*kol chai*, "all living") echoes "living soul" of 2:7
- "the ground" (*ha-'adamah*) maintained throughout 3:17, 3:19, 3:23

---

## Disagreement Log

The following verses produced significant divergences among EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA. Each divergence is analyzed and SOPHIA's resolution stated.

---

### Disagreement 1 — 3:1: Rendering of *'Arum*

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | *'arum* [cunning/shrewd] | Preserve the Hebrew term with gloss; honor the *'arummim* echo |
| LOGOS | "more crafty" | Moral ambiguity of craftiness; standard formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the most cunning" | Superlative; positive-register intelligence; predatory precision |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "more cunning" — adopts PNEUMA's word "cunning" (intelligence + predatory intent, positive-register ambiguity) combined with the MT's comparative construction *mikkol* ("more than all"), not the LXX's superlative. This keeps the Hebrew's precise comparative ("more cunning than any beast") without the LXX's over-intensification.

**Note on the untranslatable:** The *'arum*/*'arummim* wordplay (cunning/naked) bridging Gen 2:25 and 3:1 is acknowledged by all three translators as the chapter's most important literary device and is entirely untranslatable in English. SOPHIA records it here and defers to the notes rather than attempting any artifice in the translation text itself.

---

### Disagreement 2 — 3:7: *Chagorot* — Loin-Coverings vs. Coverings

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "loin-coverings [*chagorot*]" | Literal: *chagar* = to gird; anatomically precise; contrasts with 3:21 |
| LOGOS | "coverings" | Broader; functionally accurate; avoids over-specification |
| PNEUMA | "coverings" | Same as LOGOS; natural English |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "loin-coverings" — EMET's precision is correct. The word *chagorot* specifically denotes a girded lower-body covering, not a generic covering. This precision: (a) is supported by the root etymology and the LXX's *perizōmata* ("loincloths/girded coverings"); (b) creates a meaningful contrast with the *kotnot 'or* (garments of skin, 3:21), which are full tunics — the human attempt is inadequate and anatomically partial; the divine provision is comprehensive.

---

### Disagreement 3 — 3:8: *Le-ruach ha-yom* — Wind/Breeze/Cool of the Day

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in-[the]-breeze of-the-day" | Literal meteorological; *ruach* = wind/breath |
| LOGOS | "in the wind of the day" | Formal equivalence; literal *ruach* |
| PNEUMA | "in the cool of the day" | Most evocative; follows KJV; atmospheric quality of the moment |

**LXX (Vaticanus):** *to deilinon* ("in the afternoon/evening") — temporal interpretation.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "in the cool of the day" — PNEUMA's case is adopted on the grounds that: (a) the phrase describes the late-afternoon cooling wind of a Mediterranean/Levantine garden; "cool of the day" captures the experiential reality that *ruach ha-yom* names; (b) it has literary dignity in English that honors the gravity of the narrative moment; (c) EMET's "breeze of the day" and LOGOS's "wind of the day" are correct but tonally flat for this passage. The LXX's purely temporal rendering (*to deilinon*) sacrifices the atmospheric dimension and is not adopted.

**Warning — minor theological note:** "Cool of the day" follows the KJV tradition. Scholars who prefer the more literal "wind of the day" (LOGOS/EMET) have textual grounds; their position is recorded here. The wind-as-*ruach* resonance (the same word as the divine breath in 2:7) is noted by PNEUMA and is a genuine Hebrew feature this rendering cannot fully reproduce.

---

### Disagreement 4 — 3:15: *Shuph* — "Bruise" vs. "Crush"/"Strike"

| Translator | Rendering (1st occurrence) | Rendering (2nd occurrence) | Symmetric? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bruise" | "bruise" | Yes | Same verb both times; preserve Hebrew symmetry |
| LOGOS | "bruise" | "bruise" | Yes | Formal symmetry; "crush" defensible but asymmetric |
| PNEUMA | "crush" | "strike" | No | Asymmetric to reflect different lethality of head vs. heel blow |

**LXX (Vaticanus):** *tērēsei*/*tērēseis* ("shall watch for / guard against") — both instances; combat imagery replaced by mutual surveillance.

**Vulgate:** *conteret* (she shall crush) / *insidiaberis* (you shall lie in wait for) — asymmetric and uses feminine pronoun; rejected on textual grounds.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "He shall bruise your head / and you shall bruise his heel" — symmetric, following EMET and LOGOS. Reasons: (a) the Hebrew uses the same verb (*shuph*) twice; introducing English asymmetry where none exists in Hebrew oversteps the translator's mandate; (b) the asymmetry of *outcome* (head-blow is more lethal than heel-bite) is expressed through the anatomical terms "head" and "heel," which do the theological work without requiring different verbs; (c) the LXX's *tērēsei* is noted but not adopted — it is ancient and attested but replaces physical combat with watchful enmity, which is a significant interpretive departure from the MT. The MT's physical-force imagery is preferred.

**Warning — theological significance:** This verse is the *Protoevangelium* of Christian tradition and the focus of extensive Marian interpretation (based on the Vulgate's *ipsa*). SOPHIA's translation follows the MT masculine *hu'* ("he") confirmed by Vaticanus's masculine *autos*, not the Vetus Latina *ipsa* ("she"). The reading of this verse as a messianic promise is a legitimate interpretive tradition but is not embedded in the translation. The text is translated; the interpretation is left to the reader.

---

### Disagreement 5 — 3:16a: *'Itsabon* + *Herayon* — Pain and Conception

| Translator | Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "your pain and your conception" | Two separate nouns; *herayon* = pregnancy/conception |
| LOGOS | "your pain in your childbearing" | Collapses conception and birth; standard formal reading |
| PNEUMA | "your labor in childbirth severe" (paraphrase) | Omits *herayon* as separate term; dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "your pain and your conception" — EMET's rendering is adopted. The MT of the Leningrad Codex clearly distinguishes two objects of the multiplied *'itsabon*: (1) *'itsavonekh* (your pain/toil) and (2) *heronekh* (your pregnancy/conception). Collapsing them (LOGOS) or paraphrasing past them (PNEUMA) obscures a textual distinction that the manuscript supports. The Masoretic vocalization of *heronekh* is clear on folio 3r.

---

### Disagreement 6 — 3:19: *Be-ze'at 'appekha* — Nostrils/Face/Brow

| Translator | Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sweat of your nostrils [face]" | Literal: *'af* = nostril; [face] as gloss |
| LOGOS | "sweat of your face" | Idiomatic: *'appekha* = face by extension; LXX confirms |
| PNEUMA | "sweat of your brow" | Modern idiom; interpretive step beyond face |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "sweat of your face" — LOGOS's rendering is adopted. It is: (a) the idiomatic extension of the literal "nostril" that the Hebrew itself employs (*'af* → "face"); (b) confirmed by the LXX Vaticanus (*tou prosōpou sou*, "of your face"); (c) more dignified in elevated verse than EMET's "nostrils" (though EMET is technically correct). PNEUMA's "brow" is a further interpretive step that the text does not require — "face" is the standard Hebrew idiom for *'af* in this register.

---

### Disagreement 7 — 3:24: *Lahat ha-cherev* — Flame/Flaming of the Sword

| Translator | Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-flame of [the]-sword the-revolving" | *Lahat* as noun ("flame"); *mithappekhet* = "revolving" |
| LOGOS | "the flame of a sword turning itself" | *Lahat* as noun; "turning itself" for Hithpael |
| PNEUMA | "the flaming, turning sword" | *Lahat* as adjective ("flaming"); "turning" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the flame of the revolving sword" — combining EMET's noun reading of *lahat* ("the flame") with EMET's "revolving" for *mithappekhet* (better than LOGOS's "turning itself" and PNEUMA's bare "turning" for capturing the Hithpael's continuous rotational force). "The flame of the revolving sword" treats *lahat* as the governing noun (the flame, i.e., the fiery quality that accompanies the sword) and *ha-mithappekhet* as the attributive participle on the sword (the continuously revolving one). This preserves both the fire and the perpetual autonomous motion. All three translators and the LXX Vaticanus (*tēn phloginēn rhomphaian tēn strephomenēn*) agree on the substance; only the grammatical analysis of *lahat* (noun or adjective) differs.

---

*Manuscript sources confirmed: Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2v–3r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman); directly examined in GEN_003_page_001.jpg and GEN_003_page_002.jpg. Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), examined via DigiVatLib. DSS: 4QGen-b (4Q2), DJD XII.*

*The governing value of this translation is consilience — the wisest synthesis of all available evidence from all three translators, the manuscript tradition, and the ancient versions. Where EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA agreed, their agreement was taken as strong evidence. Where they diverged, the divergence was analyzed on textual, grammatical, and literary grounds and resolved with full transparency.*

— **SOPHIA** (Σοφία — Wisdom)
*Consensus arbiter, The SOPHIA Bible*
*Translation completed: 2026-03-04*
